macro_events.event_summary: Oil prices rise as U.S.-Iran tensions raise the risk of disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, through which ~20% of global oil flows.
primary_drivers: Geopolitical risk premium in crude prices
Potential supply disruption through Strait of Hormuz chokepoint
Higher input costs for fuel-intensive industries

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream production; realized prices on US/non-Gulf barrels rise with Brent without direct Hormuz transit risk. Highest earnings sensitivity per dollar of crude among US integrateds.
Same upstream price-leverage thesis as XOM; Permian and US GoM production captures higher realizations while Hormuz transit risk is minimal for its barrels.
Pure-play Permian E&P with no international exposure — captures higher WTI prices with zero Hormuz transit risk. Highest beta to crude among US large-cap E&Ps.
US-focused E&P (Permian-heavy) with high operating leverage to crude; benefits from price spike without Iran-region exposure.
Has Egyptian production but the bulk of value is US shale; smaller-cap E&P with high crude beta on geopolitical risk-on trades.
Subsea/offshore equipment provider — sustained higher crude prices accelerate offshore project FIDs and order book; classic second-derivative beneficiary of a risk premium that lingers.
Cheniere — US LNG exporter. Hormuz risk threatens Qatari LNG flows (~20% of global LNG); buyers diversify to US Gulf supply, supporting term-contract pricing and spot premiums.
International Seaways — crude tanker operator. Hormuz tensions extend voyage distances (rerouting around Cape) and spike VLCC day rates; tanker equities historically rip on Hormuz scares.

losers.rationale: Southwest has the least fuel hedging among US legacy carriers and jet fuel is ~25-30% of opex; direct EPS hit from sustained crude spike.
American Airlines — high leverage, minimal hedging, fuel is largest variable cost. Geopolitical crude spikes historically cause 3-5% intraday drawdowns in AAL.
Same fuel cost passthrough as peers; international routes also face overflight rerouting if Iran airspace closes, adding fuel burn.
Delta operates its own refinery (Trainer) which provides a partial hedge, but net exposure to higher crack spreads and crude is still negative for margins.
Fuel surcharges lag spot prices by weeks; near-term margin compression on air freight network which is fuel-intensive.
Carnival — bunker fuel is a major opex line and cruise pricing is sticky; sustained crude rise compresses margins. Also middle-east itinerary risk.

headlines: Oil rises as U.S.-Iran tensions keep traders focused on Strait of Hormuz risks
